region_of_origin: Tijuana, Mexico (origin, Caesar Cardini 1924); Singapore restaurant adaptation
food_subgroup: Salad main dish with grilled protein — Western restaurant style

ontology.foodon_label: Caesar salad (closest parent: mixed salad with protein)
ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for a prepared Caesar salad with grilled chicken as a composite restaurant dish. Closest parent is a mixed salad with protein component. URI left null to preserve ontology honesty.
ontology.foodex2_description: Salad, mixed, with meat — closest FoodEx2 parent for a composite Caesar salad with grilled chicken
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Caesar salad with grilled chicken. A0FJP (mixed salad with meat) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Caesar salad with grilled chicken as a prepared composite dish.

serving.description: 1 restaurant plate (~350g including chicken, lettuce, croutons, parmesan, dressing)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore restaurant plate of Caesar salad with grilled chicken, approximately 350g, comprising romaine lettuce, sliced grilled chicken breast, parmesan shavings, croutons, and Caesar dressing with anchovy.

nutrient_source.title: Caesar Salad with Chicken — Component Calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries (romaine lettuce FDC #169247, grilled chicken breast FDC #171477, Caesar dressing FDC #172920, parmesan FDC #173420, croutons FDC #174993)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component-weighted calculation using USDA SR Legacy entries for each ingredient at Singapore restaurant portion proportions. No single USDA entry exists for this composite dish. Values represent a weighted blend: romaine lettuce ~120g, grilled chicken breast ~100g, Caesar dressing ~50g, parmesan ~15g, croutons ~30g, anchovy ~10g, scaled to per-100g basis. Confidence: medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Salad with Protein and Fat — Analogous Estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for white bread (GI 70), romaine lettuce (negligible), and fat/protein attenuation effect on starch digestion
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Caesar salad with grilled chicken. GI estimated at ~40 using the University of Sydney GI Database as the reference framework: crouton white bread starch carries GI ~70, substantially attenuated by the high fat content of Caesar dressing (olive oil, egg emulsion) and the protein load from grilled chicken breast, consistent with published fat-protein attenuation of starch GI. GL per 350g serving = (40 × 24.15g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.7. Confidence: 0.85.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from anchovy-based Caesar dressing (~1,148mg per 350g serving, approximately 47–57% of the 2,000–2,400mg daily sodium limit)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should request Caesar dressing served on the side and use no more than half the standard portion, or ask for an anchovy-free dressing variant; the romaine lettuce and grilled chicken are low-sodium and nutritionally beneficial, but the standard dressing alone contributes ~350–450mg sodium per 2 tablespoons.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic load (~9.7 per 350g serving) driven by high protein (35g per serving) and fat content attenuating starch digestion from croutons
type2_diabetes.guidance: Caesar salad with grilled chicken is one of the most glycaemically favourable Western restaurant main dishes for people with Type 2 Diabetes, with a GL of approximately 9.7 per plate; the high protein from grilled chicken breast supports post-meal glucose stability, and the dish can be further optimised by requesting reduced croutons.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (~1,148mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~6.7g per serving) from parmesan and dressing, partially offset by olive oil monounsaturated fat and small EPA+DHA contribution from anchovy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the primary concern is sodium from the anchovy-based dressing; requesting dressing on the side and using sparingly reduces sodium substantially, while the olive oil base of the dressing and the lean grilled chicken breast are cardioprotective components that support a heart-healthy meal when sodium is managed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory components including olive oil oleocanthal (COX-1/COX-2 inhibitor), folate from romaine lettuce, and small EPA+DHA contribution from anchovy (~0.3g per serving)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Caesar salad with grilled chicken is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to anti-inflammatory components in the dressing and lettuce; patients on warfarin should note moderate vitamin K content from romaine lettuce (~100mcg per 100g) and maintain consistent intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Caesar Salad with Grilled Chicken is a Western restaurant main dish served widely across Singapore cafés and casual dining restaurants. The dish consists of crisp romaine lettuce tossed in a creamy anchovy-based Caesar dressing, topped with sliced grilled chicken breast, shaved parmesan cheese, and toasted croutons. A standard Singapore restaurant plate weighs approximately 350g and delivers around 494 kcal, 35g protein, 27g fat, and 24g carbohydrate. The glycemic load per plate is approximately 9.7 (low), making it one of the most glycaemically favourable Western restaurant main dishes available. Despite its reputation as a 'healthy salad', the dish carries approximately 1,148mg sodium per plate — primarily from the anchovy paste and Worcestershire sauce in the Caesar dressing — which exceeds the sodium content of many Singapore rice and noodle dishes. The grilled chicken breast provides lean high-quality protein, while the olive oil base of the dressing contributes monounsaturated fat. Parmesan contributes calcium (~350mg per plate) and umami depth. Croutons add texture and are the primary source of refined carbohydrate. For Type 2 Diabetes management, this dish is excellent when ordered with dressing on the side. For hypertension and cardiovascular disease, sodium management via dressing control is the key clinical intervention. The dish originated with Caesar Cardini in Tijuana, Mexico in 1924 and has been adapted into Singapore restaurant menus as a standard Western lunch option.
common_user_queries: is caesar salad good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from component-weighted calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients at estimated Singapore restaurant portion proportions. No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish as served in Singapore restaurants. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database reference framework. Confidence is medium rather than high because exact restaurant-specific ingredient ratios and dressing formulations vary.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Caesar salad dish (including dressing blend) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database; individual component fatty acid data exists but reliable weighted composite values cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported as a single value in USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy component entries for grilled chicken breast, romaine lettuce, and standard Caesar dressing formulation. Sodium per 350g serving calculated as 328mg × 3.5 = 1,148mg. GL per serving calculated as (GI 40 × 24.15g available carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 9.7. The input record noted a portion size of ~320g in the name field but ~350g in the portions array; the 350g figure from the portions array is used as the canonical serving size as it is more consistent with Singapore restaurant plating standards for a main-course salad with grilled chicken. Vitamin A reported as mcg RAE equivalent based on USDA SR Legacy retinol activity equivalent values for romaine lettuce and grilled chicken components.
